name: tracing
what_it_does: Structured, contextual logging and tracing framework for Rust. Replaces the log crate with spans (contextual groupings) and events (individual log records) that carry structured key-value fields. Integrates with async Rust (Tokio) to propagate context across .await points. Pluggable subscriber system supports output to JSON, Jaeger, Zipkin, OpenTelemetry, or terminal.
use_cases: Add structured observability to Rust agent services with spans that track LLM API calls, tool executions, and pipeline stages
Propagate trace context across async agent tasks using tracing's async-aware span system
Export agent execution traces to Jaeger or Zipkin via tracing-opentelemetry integration for distributed tracing
Log structured agent events (tool calls, model responses, latency) with key-value fields for log aggregation
Debug complex async agent pipelines by examining span hierarchies and timing in tokio-console
not_for: Simple println-style logging in scripts — the log crate or env_logger is simpler for basic text logging
Applications not using Rust — use OpenTelemetry SDKs for language-agnostic distributed tracing
Binary size-sensitive embedded systems — tracing adds non-trivial binary size overhead
best_when: You're building a production Rust async service (agent backend, HTTP API) and need structured, contextual observability that integrates with Jaeger, Zipkin, or cloud observability platforms.
avoid_when: You just need simple log output — use env_logger with the log crate for simpler setups; tracing is worth it for production services but overkill for scripts.

auth.notes: Local observability library — auth for external backends (Jaeger, OTLP) is configured in the subscriber/exporter.

pricing.notes: MIT license. Part of the Tokio project.

performance.rate_limits: No rate limits. Minimal overhead when spans are disabled via level filtering. Async-compatible — no blocking in instrumented code.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Structured events include file, line, and module path. Span context is automatically attached to events within the span. Subscriber configuration errors surface at initialization.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Observability library — no side effects on application logic. Logging/tracing is always additive.
known_agent_gotchas: tracing requires a subscriber to be installed globally (tracing_subscriber::fmt::init()) before any spans or events are recorded — events before init() are silently dropped
The #[instrument] macro creates a span for the function but async functions must have the async support enabled — use #[instrument] and ensure the function is async or use async_trait
Span fields are captured at span creation time — fields that change during the span must use span.record("field", &value) to update
Multiple subscribers can be composed with tracing_subscriber::layer() but order matters — layer precedence determines which subscriber sees events first
tracing-opentelemetry integration requires matching version alignment between tracing, opentelemetry, and tracing-opentelemetry crates — version mismatches cause compilation failures
Dynamic field values in info!(key = %dynamic_value) use Display format — use ?value for Debug format; mixing up format specifiers causes unexpected output

security_score_components.security_notes: Be careful not to log sensitive data (API keys, PII) in span fields — structured fields are exportable. Use redaction layer for production deployments handling sensitive data.
